items.evidence_text:
Advisor: Starting with income: what do you consider your household gross income in a typical year, all-in?
Client 1: Roughly like ninety grand?
Client 2: No—David, it’s not ninety. It’s about eighty-five thousand a year, like $85,150 if we’re being exact.
Client 1: Okay, yeah—$85k-ish. Margaret’s right.
Advisor: Got it—about $85,150 gross household income annually. And just so I’m clear, that’s gross, before taxes, not what hits the checking account, right?

Advisor: Okay, annual interest/dividends included in that household gross. Now spending—what do your monthly expenses run, roughly, for the household?
Client 1: Maybe like three grand a month?
Client 2: It’s higher. It’s about $3,650 a month total—mortgage, groceries, utilities, all of it.
Client 1: Yeah, okay, $3,650. I always lowball it.
Advisor: Happens all the time. So $3,650 per month in total expenses. When you say that, Margaret, is that average month, not like a holiday month?

Advisor: Starting with income: what do you consider your household gross income in a typical year, all-in?
Client 1: Roughly like ninety grand?
Client 2: No—David, it’s not ninety. It’s about eighty-five thousand a year, like $85,150 if we’re being exact.
Client 1: Okay, yeah—$85k-ish. Margaret’s right.
Advisor: Got it—about $85,150 gross household income annually. And just so I’m clear, that’s gross, before taxes, not what hits the checking account, right?

Client 1: Yeah, that’s basically the cushion.
Client 2: And it helps me sleep, honestly.
Advisor: Makes sense. Okay—before we move on, I want to sanity-check totals. If I add up investable assets—taxable $148,700, retirement $104,350, bank $38,400, and private markets $46,100—that’s about $337,550. Does that match what you think of as your investable assets total?
Client 1: Yeah, that sounds right—about $337,550.
Client 2: Yes, that’s the total we use.

Client 2: Yes—about $46,100.
Advisor: Okay, $46,100 private markets. And then your home—primary residence—what do you estimate the value at right now?
Client 1: Around $266,200.
Client 2: Yep, about $266,200.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm, do you have any loans outstanding against the house or anything else? Like a mortgage balance, HELOC, anything like that?

Client 2: No—David, it’s not ninety. It’s about eighty-five thousand a year, like $85,150 if we’re being exact.
Client 1: Okay, yeah—$85k-ish. Margaret’s right.
Advisor: Got it—about $85,150 gross household income annually. And just so I’m clear, that’s gross, before taxes, not what hits the checking account, right?

Client 1: Roughly like ninety grand?

Client 1: Yeah, that’s right—U.S. RIA.
Advisor: Perfect. Now risk—this is where people sometimes answer differently depending on the week. How would you describe your overall risk tolerance as a household?
Client 1: Aggressive.
Client 2: I’d say… moderate-aggressive.
Client 1: No, come on—Margaret, we’re aggressive. We’ve talked about this.

Client 2: Yeah, seriously. Anyway—go ahead.
Advisor: Alright—thanks. Going back to what you said earlier about keeping categories clean: I want to zoom out for a second and make sure I’ve got the household basics right before we talk investments.
Advisor: David, Margaret—are you two married, or partnered some other way?
Client 1: Married.
Client 2: Married—yeah.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Margaret—good to meet you both. I’m your advisor for today. Before we dive in, how are you two doing?
Client 1: Doing alright. Busy, but good.
Client 2: Yeah, fine—traffic was annoying, but we made it.
Advisor: I hear you. Okay, David, Margaret—tell me what you want to get out of today. Like, what would make this meeting a win?

Client 1: And it runs until—hang on—2050, I think.
Client 2: Yes, through 2050.
Advisor: Perfect. Thanks. Now, quick pause—David, earlier you said you want a coordinated plan and tax efficiency. With your income being variable, do you keep that $38,400 cash as your buffer for the business swings?
Client 1: Yeah, that’s basically the cushion.
Client 2: And it helps me sleep, honestly.

Advisor: Good question—where you live day-to-day, your home state for taxes. Residence.
Client 2: Okay, then yes—California.
Advisor: Great. Now, taxes—this is where it gets a little fuzzy sometimes. When you think about your bracket, are you roughly in the 35% federal bracket?
Client 1: I think we’re more like 32%.
Client 2: No—David, last year it was 35%.

Client 1: Doing alright. Busy, but good.
Client 2: Yeah, fine—traffic was annoying, but we made it.

Client 1: I want a clean plan. We’ve got money in a few places and I want it coordinated, and I don’t want to overpay taxes.

Client 1: I want a clean plan. We’ve got money in a few places and I want it coordinated, and I don’t want to overpay taxes.
Client 2: And I want the numbers to be right. Last time we did this with someone, it was… hand-wavy.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s anchor on the basics first—income, spending, assets, any debt, and then protection like insurance. Going back to your point about accounts being scattered, we’ll list each one and who owns it.

Client 2: Gross means before taxes. Net is what we see. So yes, $85,150 gross.
Advisor: Perfect, thanks for catching that. Now, within that, do you each have your own self-employment income amounts you track?
Client 1: Yeah. Mine’s about $67,400 gross for the year.
Client 2: And mine’s about $17,750 gross.
Advisor: Great. And I’m also seeing you have some interest and dividends—do you think of that as part of the annual income number we just said, or separate?

Client 1: Yeah, I’m the primary. I run the business day-to-day.
Client 2: He is, yes.
Advisor: And occupation-wise, David—would you describe yourself as a business owner, versus like W-2 employee or contractor?
Client 1: Business owner. Self-employed business owner.
Advisor: Perfect. One quick admin-y thing—just so I don’t mix up notes: between you two, who should I treat as “Client 1” and who’s “Client 2” in our file?

Advisor: Great. And Margaret, any private markets or alternative investment account at a brokerage in your name?
